chunk_summary: This section discusses case-based management of non-IgE gastrointestinal food allergies in infants, including FPIAP, FPIES, and FPE, with emphasis on diagnostic strategies, elimination diets, and red flags for referral.
learning_objectives: Differentiate between FPIAP, FPIES, and FPE based on clinical presentation and timing.
Understand when to initiate maternal elimination diets and their endpoints in suspected FPIAP.
Recognize red flags in chronic diarrhea and malnutrition that require referral and advanced management.
Develop appropriate action plans for FPIES, including supervised tolerance testing.
key_facts: FPIAP: Blood-streaked stools in thriving infants may resolve with anal fissure treatment; maternal dairy elimination is trialed if blood persists, with endpoints within 72 hours.
FPIES: Symptoms like repetitive vomiting, pallor, and lethargy occur 2-4 hours post-ingestion; management includes trigger avoidance and supervised tolerance testing.
FPE or chronic FPIES: Chronic diarrhea, edema, and poor weight gain with low albumin require hydration, nutritional rescue, and formula escalation (eHF to AAF).
Hospitalization is indicated for dehydration, electrolyte disturbances, or poor intake in severe cases of FPE or chronic FPIES.
Food-specific IgE testing is not used to diagnose FPIES.
reference_quote: ‘Management priorities are hydration, nutrition rescue, and a severity-based formula plan (eHF trial with rapid escalation to AAF when needed), with evaluation for infection and other causes of enteropathy.’ (Source: WAO DRACMA guideline update, 2023)
document_key_topics: Non-IgE mediated food allergies in infants
FPIAP, FPIES, and FPE diagnostic and management strategies
Nutritional and hydration management in severe cases
Role of elimination diets and referral criteria
